title: Variation — continuous vs discontinuous
examTip: AQA regularly asks you to classify examples as continuous or discontinuous, and to explain the sources of variation. Know the distinction cold, give examples for each type, and always be clear whether variation is genetic, environmental, or both.
introTips: Variation is the raw material for evolution — without differences between individuals, natural selection has nothing to work with. That's why this topic matters beyond the exam.
The continuous vs discontinuous distinction comes up in data interpretation questions too — if you're shown a histogram, that's continuous variation; a bar chart with gaps is discontinuous.
Environmental variation is easy to overlook — remember that language, accent, scars, and fitness level are all examples of variation that genes don't determine.
intro: No two people are identical — even identical twins have differences. But why do we vary? Some differences come from your genes, some from your environment, and many from a combination of both. Understanding where variation comes from is the first step to understanding how evolution works: natural selection can only act on inherited variation, so the source of a difference determines whether it can be passed on. This lesson draws the line between what's genetic, what's environmental, and how variation is categorised.

keyPoints.heading: Sources of variation — genetic and environmental
keyPoints.content: Variation between individuals of the same species has two main sources. Genetic variation comes from the random shuffling of alleles during meiosis (sexual reproduction) and from mutations — random changes to DNA that create new alleles. Every time a new organism is produced sexually, the combination of alleles is unique. Environmental variation comes from the conditions an organism experiences during its lifetime — diet, exercise, temperature, exposure to disease, education, and so on. Many characteristics are influenced by both genetic and environmental factors. Height, for example, has a strong genetic component (your genes set the potential range) but is also affected by diet and health during development.
writeBullets: Genetic variation: caused by mutations and the random shuffling of alleles during meiosis
Every sexually reproduced organism has a unique combination of alleles
Environmental variation: caused by diet, exercise, disease, climate, education, etc.
Many characteristics = both genetic and environmental influence (e.g. height, mass)
Only genetic variation can be inherited and passed to the next generation

keyTerms.def: The differences in characteristics between individuals of the same species
Variation caused by differences in allele combinations — produced by mutation and meiosis
Variation caused by factors in the environment — diet, exercise, temperature, etc. — cannot be inherited
The type of cell division that produces gametes (sex cells) — shuffles alleles to produce genetic variation

keyPoints.memTip: GE variation: Genetic (from genes, can be inherited) and Environmental (from experience, cannot be inherited). The question is: could you pass it to your children? If yes → genetic. If no → environmental.

keyPoints.heading: Continuous variation — a spectrum of differences
keyPoints.content: Continuous variation shows a range of values with no distinct categories. Characteristics like height, body mass, hand span, and foot length are all continuous — they can take any value within a range and individuals can't be clearly placed into one category or another. Continuous variation is typically influenced by many genes working together (polygenic inheritance) as well as the environment. When plotted, continuous data produces a bell-shaped (normal distribution) curve or a histogram with no gaps. Height is the classic AQA example: genetics sets the range, but diet and health during childhood affect the final outcome.
writeBullets: Continuous variation: range of values, no distinct categories
Examples: height, body mass, hand span, foot length
Data plotted as bell curve / normal distribution / histogram without gaps
Height: genetic range + diet/health effects = continuous outcome

keyTerms.def: Variation that shows a range of values with no distinct categories — e.g. height, mass
Controlled by many genes acting together — typical of continuous characteristics
A bell-shaped curve — the typical pattern for continuous variation in a population

keyPoints.memTip: Continuous = can't put people in neat boxes. You measure it with a ruler or scales, not by placing people into groups. The histogram has no gaps — the data flows from one value to the next.

keyPoints.heading: Discontinuous variation — clear categories
keyPoints.content: Discontinuous variation shows distinct, separate categories with no intermediate values. Blood group (A, B, AB, O), eye colour, tongue-rolling ability, and earlobe attachment are all examples. You either belong to one category or another — there's no 'slightly A' blood group. Discontinuous variation is mainly controlled by one or a few genes, with little or no environmental influence. When plotted, discontinuous data produces a bar chart with gaps between bars. Blood group is the classic AQA example: entirely genetic, clearly categorised, unaffected by lifestyle.
writeBullets: Discontinuous variation: distinct categories, no intermediate values
Examples: blood group (A, B, AB, O), tongue rolling, earlobe type, eye colour
Mainly controlled by one or few genes; little environmental influence
Data plotted as bar chart with gaps between bars
Blood group: entirely genetic, no environmental effect

keyTerms.def: Variation with distinct, separate categories and no intermediates — e.g. blood group, tongue rolling
A classic example of discontinuous variation — determined by a single gene with multiple alleles (A, B, O)

keyPoints.memTip: Discontinuous = you can Divide into boxes. Bar chart with gaps: each bar is one category, nothing in between. If you can say 'I am group A OR B OR AB OR O' with no middle ground — that's discontinuous.

tables.title: Continuous vs discontinuous variation
rows: Blood group, tongue rolling, earlobe type

1.question: Which of the following is an example of discontinuous variation?
options: Height in a class of students
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — ABO blood group has four distinct categories (A, B, AB, O) with no intermediate values. That's the defining feature of discontinuous variation.
1.wrongFeedback: ABO blood group is the correct answer — it has clear, separate categories with no middle ground. Height, body mass, and hand span are all continuous — they show a range of values and are plotted as a histogram or bell curve.

2.question: A student's height is affected by both genes and diet. What type of variation is height?
options: Only genetic variation — diet cannot affect height
Only environmental variation — genes have no role
Both genetic and environmental — it's continuous variation influenced by multiple factors
Discontinuous — people are either tall or short
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — height is continuous variation influenced by both genetics (sets the potential range) and environment (diet and health affect the final outcome). It's polygenic and environmentally modified.
2.wrongFeedback: Height is continuous variation with both genetic and environmental components. Your genes determine your potential height range, but diet and health during development affect where within that range you end up. Neither alone explains the full picture.

3.question: Which type of variation can be inherited by offspring?
options: Both genetic and environmental variation equally
Neither — variation is reset each generation
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — only genetic variation can be inherited because it is encoded in the DNA and passed from parent to offspring via gametes. Environmental variation (e.g. scars, fitness level) is not encoded in DNA and cannot be inherited.
3.wrongFeedback: Only genetic variation can be inherited. Environmental changes (a scar, learned skills, muscle built from exercise) are not stored in DNA and cannot be passed to offspring. This distinction is fundamental to understanding natural selection.

commonMistakes: Saying eye colour is continuous variation — it's discontinuous. Eyes are categorised as blue, green, brown, hazel etc., not measured on a continuous scale.
Confusing the graph types — continuous variation → histogram or bell curve; discontinuous variation → bar chart with gaps. Examiners test this with data questions.
Forgetting that environmental variation cannot be inherited — this is the critical point for evolution. Natural selection only acts on genetic variation because only DNA is passed to offspring.
Saying height is purely genetic — it has a strong genetic component, but diet, illness, and exercise also matter. AQA expects you to acknowledge both.

Describing all mutations as causing disease — mutations are the source of new alleles and therefore new genetic variation. Most are neutral, and occasionally they produce beneficial variation that natural selection can favour.
examTips: When classifying variation, ask two questions: (1) Can you measure it on a scale (continuous) or put it in clear categories (discontinuous)? (2) Is it mainly genetic, mainly environmental, or both?
For graph questions: a histogram with no gaps = continuous variation; a bar chart with gaps = discontinuous. This is a data skills question as much as a biology one.
When explaining why environmental variation cannot be inherited, say clearly: 'Environmental variation is not encoded in DNA and therefore cannot be passed to offspring in gametes.'

revisionCardBullets: Variation = differences in characteristics between individuals of the same species
Two sources: genetic (from mutations + meiosis) and environmental (diet, exercise, climate)
Only genetic variation can be inherited — environmental changes don't alter DNA
Many characteristics are influenced by both (e.g. height, body mass)
Continuous variation: range of values, no distinct categories (e.g. height, mass)
Continuous data → histogram or bell curve; polygenic + environmental influence
Discontinuous variation: clear categories, no intermediates (e.g. blood group, tongue rolling)
Discontinuous data → bar chart with gaps; mainly one or few genes, little environmental effect
Genetic variation arises from: mutations + random allele shuffling during meiosis
Natural selection acts only on inherited (genetic) variation — environmental variation cannot be selected for
